steps.title: Identify the blank's role in the sentence
steps.content: The sentence reads: "chemistry ______ and his colleagues at Temple University recently developed a new approach." The blank fills the space between "chemistry" and "and his colleagues" — it must complete the compound subject. The question is whether commas belong between the title and the name, or after the name.

steps.title: Test each choice for correct title-name punctuation
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'professor Yugang Sun': title flows directly into name with no comma, forming a single uninterrupted noun phrase as part of the compound subject "chemistry professor Yugang Sun and his colleagues." ✓
  • Choice B — 'professor Yugang Sun,': the trailing comma after "Sun" separates the subject from the conjunction "and his colleagues," breaking the compound subject. ✗
  • Choice C — 'professor, Yugang Sun': the comma between the title "professor" and the name "Yugang Sun" incorrectly treats them as two separate elements rather than a title-name unit. ✗
  • Choice D — 'professor, Yugang Sun,': commas both before and after the name set it off as a parenthetical appositive, which breaks the compound subject structure. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
